After more consideration, I don't think this is the implementation we want for several reasons.

# Encrypting the entire file isn't necessary. It's convenient, to be sure, because there's only one cipher text and consequently, only one secret key to protect it. However, it is the goal of the Crypto keyring to protect the passwords and not other data in the file.
# Encrypting the entire file means the file can't be managed as plaintext (such as with diff or a revision control system). It can't be hand-edited (such as to remove passwords), and it can't have application-specific data (comments, other sections, and entries) added to the file, as it could with the previous implementation.
# By not subclassing from the BasicFileKeyring, the implementations of BasicFileKeyring and PlainTextKeyring don't make any sense (they speak of encryption but nothing ever implements any). Furthermore, there is a lot of overlap between BasicFileKeyring and Crypto keyring, but the code was copied, rather than factored into a common class.
# By removing the tests for encrypt/decrypt, we actually have less test coverage than we did on the previous implementation.
# Because the entire file is decrypted into memory, this means that even passwords that are not being used are loaded into memory during the encryption or decryption step, which should be avoided if possible.

I recognize that the motivation to use a single encrypted plaintext came from a suggestion in the [[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/python-keyring/+bug/1004845|LaunchPad bug]]. I appreciate the suggestions, but I worry that the implementation causes some undesirable effects.

So I've started work on a new implementation that takes the spirit (and I hope security) of Sebastian's approach, producing a unique salt and IV for each password, plus a reference password for verifying the keyring password.

Original comment by: Jason R. Coombs

As is indicated by the error, the keyring library was unable to detect a suitable backend in which to store passwords.

The issue is that Heroku runs on Linux and thus you need to configure a backend. There's a section in the readme on [how to configure headless Linux systems](https://github.com/jaraco/keyring#using-keyring-on-headless-linux-systems). Unfortunately, you may not in the Heroku environment have enough control to make that happen.

Your best bet is probably to `pip install` [keyrings.alt](https://pypi.org/project/keyrings.alt), which I think will enable the plaintext keyring by default.

I'm well aware that timestamp formats *and* the timestamp formatting speed is a problem. It always was. 

It's unfortunate that DATE_FORMATS is a global variable and I don't especially like the idea of changing global variables. But what can be done is to change the parser, to have it's own copy of DATE_FORMATS, then you just need to update it as you like

    parser = gpxpy.GPXParser(xml_or_file, parser=parser)

Hey, thanks for reporting this. I've looked into this and here's what I found: When a query is cached, TinyDB returns the list of the cached results:

```python
        if cond in self._query_cache:
            return self._query_cache[cond]
```

Now, as default with Python, we return a *reference* to the cache list. When your code calls `elements.extend(...)`, it operates *on the cache list*. Basically, you add stuff to the query cache. If you copy the results first (e.g. by using `elements = db.search(query_impl)[:]`), it works as expected.

I'd say that TinyDB should handle this case better as modifying the results list of a search shouldn't mess up the query cache. The fix is fairly simple (see a1fa290dce14cea88f0438e0c2e61c019965c723), we now just copy the result list before returning it. This should fix the problem.

I ran into this issue and I think that the issue is in the iterator protocol implemented by IntervalFile; this issue is now fixed since BedTool iterators are now derived from IntervalIterator. 

Specifically, python's iterator protocol requires "that once an iterator’s next() method raises StopIteration, it will continue to do so on subsequent calls". IntervalFile's iterator does not. 

This property is used by the [itertools.groupby code](https://docs.python.org/2/library/itertools.html#itertools.groupby) to determine when to quit. The first time IntervalFile throws StopIteration (in _grouper), it closes the intervalFile_ptr. However, groupby.next tries to call next on IntervalFile's iterator again, and then it will attempt to read from the now closed file and segfault on GetNextBed (_bedStream->good()).

Pls keep in mind also: all this means that there is a limited amount of `NetlinkSocket` (the lowes API level) objects, for which the `bind()` call can be done successfully.

Thus, for long-running processes it is better to keep a permanent IPDB object or set of objects.

It is valid for all pyroute2 versions on any kernel. Previous behaviour masked this issue with a hidden port leakage. The simplest demo code:
```
from pyroute2 import IPRoute

for x in range(2000):
    if x % 10 == 0:
        print(x)
    ip = IPRoute()
    ip.bind()
    ip.close()
```
It will fail after 1024 `bind()` calls anyway, even on 3th kernels.

>How exactly is the cost calculated herein? As sum of the move values, that resulted in the minimum distance?

Yep pretty much! (https://github.com/pierre-rouanet/dtw/blob/master/dtw.py#L27)

In this implementation, there is no additional cost for adding or removing elements. It's directly the element by element comparison of the two series.
There is also some weighting over the whole path (see https://github.com/pierre-rouanet/dtw/issues/2 for more details on this).

Those choices are kind of arbitrary, you can find many different implementations of DTW depending on the data you want to compare.

I don't really work on dtw anymore but I'll gladly integrate other implementation!

Just create a custom mergetool for it:

```
[merge]
    tool = nvr

[mergetool "nvr"]
    cmd = "nvr -cc tabnew -o \"$BASE\" \"$REMOTE\" -O \"$LOCAL\" \"$MERGED\""
```

Then, when you have a conflict and you're in `:terminal`, just use `git mergetool`.

The custom command is really ugly and you probably want to customize it further, but it should get you started.

From `git help mergetool`:

```
           When git mergetool is invoked with this tool (either through the -t
           or --tool option or the merge.tool configuration variable) the
           configured command line will be invoked with $BASE set to the name
           of a temporary file containing the common base for the merge, if
           available; $LOCAL set to the name of a temporary file containing
           the contents of the file on the current branch; $REMOTE set to the
           name of a temporary file containing the contents of the file to be
           merged, and $MERGED set to the name of the file to which the merge
           tool should write the result of the merge resolution.
```

If you're willing to use Cython for the low-level parts, then I've already done some work with creating fast scanners. One of my parser implementations has an fairly optimized Cython version (although I'm new at Cython and it certainly could be improved) and an equivalent pure-Python version (they both pass the same unit tests). My current benchmarks (for the same JSON example and same number of iterations) are shown in the table below. Some explanation:

###### tests
* function composition - parser constructed using Python objects directly
* grammar definition - parser is composed from reading a grammar definition
* alt grammar def - grammar definition is similar to the fastest Parsimonious test

###### method
* match - parsing performs semantic actions and returns the data structure described by the JSON
* scan - parsing only says if the input is valid JSON, and where it stopped parsing

###### implementations
* python (int) - scanners only return an int (endpos or error code)
* python (tuple) - scanners return a tuple of (endpos, status)  (status is error or success code)
* cython - scanners return a C struct of (endpos, status); speeds are similar to only returning an int

test                 | method | python (int) | python (tuple) | cython
-------------------- | ------ | -------- | -------- | ------
function composition | match  | 1.56     | 2.28     | **0.17**
function composition | scan   | 0.92     | 1.84     | 0.06
grammar definition   | match  | 1.88     | **2.63** | 0.23
grammar definition   | scan   | 1.13     | 2.01     | 0.10
alt grammar def      | match  | 1.13     | 1.73     | 0.54
alt grammar def      | scan   | 1.07     | 1.62     | 0.52

The "python (tuple)" implementation is perhaps closes to Parsimonious, since the tuple is sorta like returning a Node object. I'm not sure if you consider my match() or scan() methods closer to your match(), since I don't return an AST by default. I've bolded the slowest match() speed (which is slower than Parsimonious), and the fastest (which is only about 3x slower than Python's hand-written json module (0.05, implemented in C), although I don't my parser doesn't unescape unicode).

Also note how much the pure-python implementation was hurt by constructing tuples for every scan, rather than just returning an int.

Anyway I think this analysis is getting beyond the scope of this particular issue, so if you're interested in merging efforts here, maybe we should have separate issue?

I tried using py2exe to build a simple application and I ran into obstacle after obstacle.

The latest py2exe doesn't run on Python 3.6 and doesn't run on Python 2.7, so I had to install Python 3.4.

After doing that, I created this [minimal script](https://gist.github.com/d69427d72b89bc18b8f1858f68c5b4dc) for building an executable that does nothing more than import cherrypy.

I installed the latest py2exe and cherrypy into the Python 3.4 system environment and then ran the script, but it crashes with an [infinite recursion](https://gist.github.com/e376cb79577d8b701885abd03261661c).

So even without involving any namespace packages, py2exe is unstable and eroding. I'm not going to be able to solve this issue. If there are issues with namespace packages in py2exe, those issues need to be published and solved in py2exe. If there's something a namespace package needs to do to support namespace packages, that technique needs to be published (at least on StackOverflow and preferably with the project).

If I do try to [build an executable that simply imports jaraco.classes](https://gist.github.com/15a27d9853efcd8735a77679637b708f), that will [run but fail to find the classes module](https://gist.github.com/4f0278999f1662867f3b80d2217e80d6).

No amount of py2exe support in `jaraco.__init__` is going to help there, because pip removes that file when installing. Perhaps there's a solution installing with eggs, but those are deprecated also.

I just don't see anything that `jaraco.*` or any other namespace package can do at this time to fix py2exe. I'm open to any suggestions.

It is because of the floating point error caused by division inside the `frange` function. It seems that  python rounds the floating points to the nearest integer value while using `<` and `>` operators, even the floating point is in python's precision range.

What I observed is that it sometimes incorrectly determines the result for less than and bigger than operators when the floating point number has 5 fractional digits on a 64bits python distribution regardless of the library or the wrapper used.

I also observed the same problem in determining `delta` from `sample_size` property. If the sample_size = 50, then `frange` fails to generate the last knot (or the set of last rows - columns) for the evaluation. If sample_size = 51, it just works fine.

I am completely open to the new `frange` implementations (without using Numpy) or some ideas on fixing the floating point error.

A fix for both of these edges might be for cme to issue private URLs (e.g. https://cme.box/<hash>) to match host responses on instead of name/ip.

You may be hit by rate-limiting policies on the server, requiring you to slow down the rate of requests against the server, or you may simply be hitting the server with expensive requests that put a strain on the server. These are the typical scenarios where I have seen `ErrorServerBusy` popping up.

You can paper over these issues by using a `ServiceAccount` instead of the `Credentials` class. See https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib#setup-and-connecting

Also, if you only intend to use some specific fields of a contact, you can use `.only()` to greatly reduce the volume and complexity of the request.

Hi @pmahata ,

The issue seems to be almost the same as #15.

The error occurs when you use two different ODBC drivers, an old one that doesn't support `datatime2` type and a new one that supports it.  

I guess you're using `SQL Server` (WDAC ODBC) driver which is bundled with Windows by default and doesn't support `datetime2` type for your application, while your database has been migrated using an another ODBC driver that supports `datetime2` type.

I recommend that you install `Microsoft ODBC Driver for SQL Server on Windows` which supports `datetime2` type and use it for your application instead of WDAC ODBC driver for better functionality and performance.

I've also made the fix for the error and pushed it to PyPI. So you don't necessarily need to upgrade your ODBC driver to resolve the error if you update django-pyodbc-azure to the latest one. But the fix is just a fallback for legacy ODBC drivers and I strongly recommend that you upgrade your ODBC driver to a newer one to run your Django application on Windows.

> but the extra complexity on installation is a bit of a downer

Not to mention npm, et al. I hear you there. We're still working on making that simpler for all involved: developers, package maintainers, and of course notebook authors and their users.

> 4.1.0 to 4.2.1

Well, the big focus of 4.2 was making extensions more reasonable, providing more robust mechanisms for adding front-end and back-end components. Also, a lot of work went into the widgets during that time, as they are simultaneously supporting both the 4.x Notebook and new JupyterLab work. I don't know what to say there: it's non-trivial to support both, and in the case of `conda`/`virtualenv`, requires a tremendous amount of hackery to support 4.1.

> save my Notebook as HTML, the widget doesn't display

That is still a work in progress. There is a mechanism for providing something which falls back to a CDN, but I don't think the big-ol-slug-of-embedded-js-and-css-and-fonts works yet, or is in the cards, but then I haven't done a lot of work around it. The new widgets do have some embedding functionality, but I haven't seen it work directly in the notebook JSON.

> Bokeh plots save to HTML without issue. Is there a trick to this, or a whole different approach

Right, the bokeh approach is backed (by default) by CDN, but also has the ability actually embed its 1mb of code as HTML in the notebook. I don't know how best to reproduce this with the widgets.

All right, I've had a chance to dig into how this widget system works and I think I've got a good handle on it now. 

It is indeed much nicer to be able to load all the JS and render elements directly in the Notebook DOM without an iframe/saving things to disk but the extra complexity on installation is a bit of a downer: for example just installing for development upgraded Jupyter for me from 4.1.0 to 4.2.1, I assume because of the `ipywidgets>=5.1.5` dependency.

On thing which I'm disappointed by is that when I save my Notebook as HTML, the widget doesn't display... I had hoped that the widget system would make this "just work" similarly to how interactive Bokeh plots save to HTML without issue. Is there a trick to this, or a whole different approach?

Oh, my apologies, I did not properly read that part of the title. This is the intended behaviour - if your application raises an error it is killed immediately and doesn't get a chance to handle any more messages. If you want to handle things in a less dramatic way, you'll need to add error handling to the methods where the error might happen.

(We have to do this because if an error gets up to the server the coroutine has exited, and we'd have to make a new instance of the application to handle any more messages, which would have different things on `self` anyway)

If you're terminating SSL at the load balancer, then you'll need to have that handle signalling for SSL domains, much the same as you would in normal Django. Specifically, it needs to both send a header (usually X-Forwarded-Proto) and then Django should have the setting SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER configured to match (I think - you should look that up in the docs)

@mkolod If I have understood the puzzle correctly, you want to access the TRT graph in the executor from frontend only for the testing purpose, right? We faced the similar situation in the subgraph development and here is how we dealt with it. I'm not implying it's the way should be, but just sharing some thoughts. I agree that from the user level, they don't need to see the TRT graph. So in the front end, the symbol is the original graph. In the graph executor in the backend, before binding, we copy the original symbol, partition the copy, and save it in the data member `graph_` of `GraphExecutor`. For the testing purpose, we add one C API exposing the partitioned graph in the graph executor to the frontend so that we can plot it in TensorBoard for checking topology. We should make it clear that the C API is for testing the partitioning algorithm, rather than a user-facing API.

Here is one question for me to understand your algorithm on breaking the cycles effectively. Taking the following network for example, A, B, C, D are four operators, and there are four directed edges:
`A->B, B->C, A->D, D->C`
Let's say A, B, C are TRT compatible, but D is not. If A, B, C were grouped into a single subgraph node E, E and D would be mutually dependent. How is the cycle detected and broken in this case? Thanks.

Not today, no.  I've been resisting adding it because this whole feature is working around an anti-pattern; you should keep your build stuff out of the source tree.

While you may think that I'm opinionated about this (I am a bit!) there is a technical reason to avoid it: some operating systems (macOS and Windows) recursively watch the directory tree and deliver change notifications through to watchman anyway.

macOS has limited support for ignoring directories: it has a fixed limit of 8 paths that must be specified at the time that the watch is established.  There is no provision for pattern matching.

This means that there is a performance penalty for keeping your build artifacts in the tree.  The more there are and the more frequently they change, the harder your system is going to have to work to simply ignore those files.

So my recommendation would be: try to get those files moved out of your source tree, or if that isn't possible, put them under a single path at the root that you can ignore; eg: rather than `foo/build` and `bar/build`, have `build/foo` and `build/bar`.

You can then use a single `ignore_dir` entry for them, and get the (second best) possible performance on macOS.

On the preferred key type: the OpenID Connect specs only list JWKs, and all providers that implement the Discovery extension will publish a JWK set (http://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-discovery-1_0.html#ProviderMetadata, e.g. for Fedora at https://id.fedoraproject.org/openidc/Jwks).
The jwks_uri is also mentioned in the core OpenID Connect specifications: http://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#RotateSigKeys

It is technically possible indeed to transform keys from PEM to JWK, but I would personally suggest that we just have people insert JWKs since that's what the specs say.

This feature request has actually been implemented as of boto/botocore#936. It is still recommended that you use properly configured clients to avoid extra requests being made however.

The error you are getting back is coming from the service. The authorize ingress API has a couple of different ways to grant permissions. I would recommend sticking with the specifying your ``CidrIp``'s and any other configuration in the list of ``IpPermissions``. Based off the [docs](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#EC2.SecurityGroup.authorize_ingress), the top-level configuration parameters are older parameter that are really only supported for pre-default VPC (i.e. EC2-Classic) and in general does not work in combination with the ``IpPermissions`` parameter.

In terms of the rule still getting propogated when an error, that is also a server-side behavior. Might be worth opening up a thread on the [AWS Developer Forums](https://forums.aws.amazon.com/forum.jspa?forumID=30) about this discrepancy.

```python
def __init__(self, source, record=None):

    self.schema = load_schema('hep')

def add_language(...):
    # do whatever it is doing now
    validate(self.record['languages'], self.schema['properties']['languages'])
```

Now I believe the correct option should be to drop `AI_ADDRCONFIG` altogether. The reason for using `AI_ADDRCONFIG` is to ensure that you're not resolving *only* IPv6 addresses on a machine that doesn't have IPv6 connection to outside, or IPv4 on a machine with no IPv4 connectivity. Since *all* of the resolved addresses are bound to, it doesn't matter whether they're internal or external. The only case is if you have *only* IPv6 addressing inside your network with no IPv4 addressing within the network and only IPv4 addressing *outside* and you're binding by a hostname, or you have IPv6 completely disabled on your system, even on localhost, but getaddrinfo still offering IPv6 address for loopback interface.

The duplicate output is expected, it depends on your local resolver and setup whether it prints the hostname, or the IP address.

In the first case, you have two sockets bound to localhost, and both are correctly resolved.

> Also is there a shorthand that can be used for ipv6 support like simply listen = localhost:6543?

Yep, that will listen on both IPv4 and IPv6 depending on what your system returns to `getaddrinfo()`. On OS X, localhost also returns the IPv6 link-local address for loopback:

- `::1`
- `fe80::1%lo0`
- `127.0.0.1`

As the three interfaces that are bound to (in that order). For example, my local machine is named "sterling.local", `getaddrinfo()` returns the following to bind to:

To reflect on the error:

1. ```
pip._vendor.packaging.requirements.InvalidRequirement: Invalid requirement, parse error at "'+git@git'"
```
is a lie.  This **is not** an invalid requirement.  This is you failing to parse a valid requirement.
2. To others who suggested that it should be something else, like `ssh+git@...` or `ssh://...` and so on: no.  It shouldn't.  This is a supported scheme, it doesn't mean the same as `ssh://` one.  In fact, it's a different protocol, completely independent of SSH.

That's right. This change was made intentionally as part of the Setuptools 34 release - dropping support for self install and upgrade. We've removed the hack that vendors setuptools dependencies. You'll want to install the dependencies with pip first. See bootstrap.py for one technique for installing the dependencies.

Rationale :

A user set PYTHONPATH in a shell environment.
He can also start other shells ( with his PYTHONPATH set a different way ).

Having the installed files content depending on PYTHONPATH makes the
install quite brittle : the installed environment will work as expected
with that PYTHONPATH value only, and not with a different one.

Use case :

I am using Python with ROS (http://www.ros.org) , which entails a lot of
different shells, potentially with different setups...

ROS uses PYTHONPATH heavily to build an environment from multiple install
prefix. So manipulating PYTHONPATH is common place.

Also ROS still relies on distutils, and custom "__init__.py" interpreting
files in other places to achieve the "editable install" effect.

I am currently working to bring ROS usage of python up to date, as much as
I can, by relying on setuptools and the current way to do things in Python.

In that context a user creates an editable install, and then run it
multiple times, from different shells with potentially different
configurations.


I will post here again when I can reproduce only with setuptools. It s
likely also that the version that we are using impacts the behavior as ROS
is still using python 2.7 and I was testing on ubuntu trusty where the
system python setup is quite old.

At the moment there isn't a way to "turn off" logging. However, you do have a few options:

* Provide a `file` object for `logFile` _(easiest)_

``` python
# ...
my_log = open('my_log.log', 'ab')
router.run('0.0.0.0', 8080, my_log)
```

* Use the "Twisted way" and don't start Twisted logs

``` python
from klein import Klein
from twisted.web.server import Site
from twisted.internet import reactor, endpoints

router = Klein()
# ...
web_server = endpoints.serverFromString(reactor, "tcp:8080")
web_server.listen(Site(router.resource()))
```

* Inherit from `Klein` and override `run` without `log.startLogging(logFile)`

@rubenbouman In the case of the `SimpleConsumer`, the [failure](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/fd8e89347bc48c2de7a9bc9a68dab5c5a84ef544/pykafka/cluster.py#L304) of the `MetadataRequest` can be caught by client code. In this case, the `SimpleConsumer` should be cleaned up, but the `KafkaClient` shouldn't need to be reinstantiated.

It's less clear to me what you're seeing in the `Producer` case. The `Producer` is designed to notice connection problems that render it unable to continue message production. However, it does attempt to continue producing messages if some but not all brokers are inaccessible - maybe this is what you're noticing?

For both of these cases, the most durable way to monitor connection status independent of production/consumption is probably to examine `[broker.connected for _, broker in KafkaClient.brokers]`, calling `client.update_cluster()` when there are any brokers not connected.

Hope that helps - I'll keep this ticket open as a reminder to document this a bit more clearly and in a [central location](http://pykafka.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html).

Actually, that statement is not correct. If the arguments have an underscore they are **not** included in the forwarded `kwargs` it is everything else that becomes part of `kwargs`. ( https://github.com/materialsproject/fireworks/blob/a2fad1df72a1c9e8c9ef3d984425d39037f71623/fireworks/user_objects/firetasks/script_task.py#L168-L171 )

Regardless, I'm still not thrilled with this as it means if you have a function taking underscored arguments they will be skipped (surprise!). Passing a dictionary is safe in comparison.

If you are concerned with breaking the API, this can be addressed by adding `PyWrapTask` or something similar to implement this behavior.

The only way I knew about this was digging into the code. I think that at a minimum there *should* be some documentation about this ( https://pythonhosted.org/FireWorks/pytask.html?highlight=pytask ).

I m pretty sure that the current implementation can do anything what you need. But you need to think a bit different about how to use the z3c.form concept. If you like to provide a form for anythig else then the object traversal context. Or let's say for a context which provides another "object" you lik to edit etc. you can simply provide a form and update/render them with your custom "content" in your view.

The context, request is a well defined concept in zope 3. The context its the traversable object where the view (page or form) adapts together with the request. It's a multi adapter on (context, request). There is nothing confusing about that. 

What we often do is to write a form for manage a (context, request) and call this form in a simply browser page like:

class ComplexForm(BrowserPage):
    """Complex form"""
    def update(self):
        content = self.getMyContentFromSomwhere()
        self.form = MoreComplexForm(content, self.request)
        self.form.update()
    super(ComplexForm, self).update()

    def render(self):
        return self.form.render()

This allows you to separate the traversal context and the form manipulating context aka content.

I guess your problem is not a z3c.form problem, It's simply a miss match between the view traversal and form context.

I'm with icemac on this one.

The flake8 changes alone would probably be fine in a separate PR, though for Zope code a global E301 exception makes more sense to me. We have these security related pseudo decorators all over the place and adding a comment to each of them adds a lot of line noise.

isort in general seems to add little value with a lot of bookkeeping. I honestly prefer to stick to the rough PEP8 guideline of separating imports into three groups, as that is what most of the code base does. Otherwise the general principle of adjusting to what you find in the file seems to be a good enough approach. The Zope Toolkit code style guidelines are also fairly silent on this point (http://zopetoolkit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/codingstyle/index.html). Though those never really applied to Zope 2 related code, they seem to make sense to me.

When we adopted flake8/pep8 it added a lot of whitespace/reformatting commits. Those were already painful, but I think they actually help readability and ongoing code maintenance. I can't say the same about isort. It adds another large set of commits/changes without really aiding code readability. And as we've seen with some of those attempts, there are imports in Zope files for compatibility purposes all over the place, and removing "unused" imports is fairly tricky.

At this point, I'd rather we focus our effort and time on porting to Python 3 and not spend our time arguing about import sorting.

p4a's api requires targeting a bootstrap, but this is actually compatible with what you want since a bootstrap is just a java android project with the python stuff prebuilt. The `service_only` bootstrap is ideal, as it doesn't by default build anything except python itself.

After building such a bootstrap, you can extract  the python project with something like `p4a export-dist --dist-name "yourdistname" output_dir`, then copy everything you need from there. You'd also need to replicate some of the unpacking that p4a does, I can give some suggestions about that if it isn't clear to you how to do so.

If you just need Python itself, a simple alternative is to use the CrystaX NDK.

> As long as the factor timestamps is a subset of the price timestamps, a given factor timestamp can always find its corresponding entry price, and thus the corresponding forward return.

I agree but there are two subtleties to consider:
1. the forward returns a factor needs are the ones computed starting from factor date index, we don't need the forward returns computed for every date in `prices`. Given that one of the point of this issue is to save computation time we don't want to increase the computation required for the common scenario of a single factor. In the intraday example if we computed the forward returns for each date in prices index we would increase the computation time by 3 times (prices index is 3 times larger than factor index) and the memory required too.
2. Depending on the factor index used to compute the forward returns, we might end up with different time periods. For example if we look at the intraday NB, the time periods inferred 1h, 3h, 1D, 2D would be different for a factor with a different date index e.g. a factor which is computed at 12:30 instead of 9:30.

> Is it a round-trip here? Is full_idx always equals prices_idx? Am I missing anything?

We don't know if `prices` is correctly populated, it might miss some dates from `factor`. To be correct in the computation we perform a `union` of the two indices instead of relying on the correctness of `prices`.

as mentioned i also think it would be useful to only have one place where we parse n3... #503 fixes `util.from_n3()`'s parsing of literals with datatypes, and allows parsing PrefixedNames by introducing a `NamespaceManager`.

Still, as `util.from_n3()` is a "single term parser", it for example doesn't really seem to do well with parsing quoted graphs.

I think some of the points to decide here are:
 - [ ] should we unify the parsing of n3?
   - [ ] should we use `parser/n3.py` code from `util.from_n3()` or
   - [ ] vice versa?
 - [ ] should we be able to parse all `rdflib.term`s from n3, e.g. `Variable`, `QuotedGraph`?

I've implemented a first version of the feature you request in https://github.com/BlueBrain/eFEL/pull/132
This has been pushed to master, and available on PyPi as version 3.0.3

The feature is called 'initburst_sahp'.
I've test that use your three traces, and they visually return the voltages that are expected.
It will only detect the first burst.

You can see the implementation here:
https://github.com/BlueBrain/eFEL/blob/master/efel/pyfeatures/pyfeatures.py#L91

I've made some initial changes to the eFEL to allow for features defined in pure python. For now all these features go in the file mentioned above. The getFeatureValues call stays the same, it works for both cpp features and python features.

This feature could be a bit unstable because it's difficult to implement this for the general case.
You can control it by setting: (use efel.setDoubleSetting, bluepyopt also allows you to set these settings).
initburst_freq_threshold: frequency in Hz, if an ISI is lower than this frequency it break the burst
initburst_sahp_start: time after last spike of burst when sahp interval starts
initburst_sahp_end: time after last spike of burst when sahp interval ends, if there is a spike before this is reached, that spike time will become the end. The same for the stimulus end.

What are the complexities we need to sort out?

Your recommendation is actually how the project was previously structured and we finally gave in and created an org with a `uap-core` that each language version embeds. The reality is that this works better for maintenance/issues/coordination. The downside is maintenance of each individual repo.

For caching:

- The pickle cache must not be versioned. It can be packaged and deployed, but not be versioned in the source.
- If we choose not to systematically validate, we'll validate on the first exception in the constructors and output either the validation result or a recommendation to open an issue (as an exception with no validation error means there is most probably an issue in the core itself).
- A separate command for validation can be provided, or an option in all CLI commands.

The current options seem to be:

- Caching: 50 ms.
- Parsing with `libyaml`: 500 ms.
- Parsing with `pyyaml`: 3 000 ms.
- Parsing & validation with `libyaml`: 8 500 ms.
- Parsing & validation with `pyyaml`: 12 000 ms.

In which case, I favour _optional validation_ and _recommendation to install `libyaml`_ over _caching_, as caching means we have to design deployment and, worst of all, cache invalidation.

original node without warning the user since the overhead would be minimal
(well, hopefully minimal). One simple way to do this: in addition to a
node's batching signature, each candidate node would need to have a "what
am i computing?" signature which would be computed recursively with a
forward-like computation. We could even build in some logic for recognizing
the identity of things like (x1 + x2) and (x2 + x1). Obviously there are
limits- even something as simple as ((x1 + x3) - x2) vs ((x1 - x2) + x3)
would be hard to detect.

On Thu, Jun 16, 2016 at 8:48 PM Bill Prin <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> Currently the Monitoring client does not support writing timeseries
> values, though that's probably the canonical use case of the API.
>
> @rimey <https://github.com/rimey> is planning on adding it, I was hoping
> to add some samples sooner so he said I could start to spec it out, and
> whoever gets to it first will do the PR, but either way we should come to
> some consensus on the usage patterns first.
>
> I know he was also concerned about #1732
> <https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/issues/1732> since
> datetimes are pretty important in monitoring, but I'm not sure yet what's
> actionable. We should definitely minimize needs for user to play with
> timestamps.
>
> Anyway high level overview of Monitoring API from my understanding
>
> MetricDescriptor - This object describes a type of Metric, can be
> parametrized with LabelDescriptors
> MonitoredResourceDescriptor - This object describes a type of thing to be
> monitored, can be parametrized with LabelDescriptors
>
> Metric - A MetricDescriptor parametrized with labels
> Resource - A ResourceDescriptor paramettrized with labels
>
> TimeSeries - Metric + Resource + points. The points have intervals,
> although for GAUGE metrics (one of the MetricKind) that interval will have
> the same start and end time and usually be the current time. CUMULATIVE
> metrics will have a separate start and end time, with the end time usually
> being the current time. DELTA metrics can not be used for custom metrics,
> so I think can be ignored for the purpose of writing timeseries, because I
> think you only ever write custom metrics (non-custom metrics are the
> default ones written for you).
>
> See the current usage docs
> <http://googlecloudplatform.github.io/gcloud-python/stable/monitoring-usage.html>
> for more context.
>
> I think the most common use case will be to write a custom GAUGE metric
> for the current time.
>
> `
> from gcloud import monitoring
> client = monitoring.Client()
> Create a Metric by its MetricDescriptor type name, assign labels as a dict Possibly
> doesn't exist but we won't check yet, unless they reload()
>
> gauge_metric = client.metric('custom.googleapis.com/my_metric',
> labels={'response_code': 404})
> Create a Resource by its resource name, assign labels as dict
>
> resource = client.resource('gce_instance', labels={'instance_id':
> 'my-instance', 'zone': 'us-central1-f'})
> Create and save the timeseries resource
>
> client.write_timeseries(metric=metric, resource=resource, points=[1,2,3])
> # API call
> GAUGE metrics should only specify end_time if now is not correct,
> start_time will default to end_time
>
> client.write_timeseries(metric=metric, resource=resource, [4,5],
> end_time=get_a_time())
>
> cumulative_metric = client.metric('
> custom.googleapis.com/my_cumulative_metric')
> end time always defaults to now
>
> cilent.write_timeseries([7, 8, 9], start_time=get_start_time()) # API call
> `
>
> I was originally thinking something like
>
> `
> Abandoned pattern Add 3 time series points, all default to interval of
> start/end at now
>
> timeseries = monitoring.TimeSeries(metric=metric, resource=resource)
> timeseries.add_points([1, 2, 3])
> Write the 3 time series values
>
> timeseries.save() # API call
> `
>
> The disadvantage is that when you want to add more points to the same
> Metric+Resource, you either have to create a new TimeSeries object anyway,
> or have strange semantics where you add points to an object you already
> created, even though really a new object is being created. So I think it's
> better if TimeSeries are immutable client-side and you just create them
> directly with the call on the client.
>
> Another small point is the start_time and end_time defaults. The idea is
> that end_time always defaults to now, unless specified, and start_time
> always defaults to end_time, unless specified. This makes sense, though it
> might look slightly strange that sometimes we are only explicitly setting
> start_time (to indicate when a CUMULATIV metric starts) and other times
> only explicitly setting end_time (to change the entire interval for a GAUGE
> metric). I think it's ok though.
>
> One of the big questions is how much client-side validation do we want to
> do. For example given a GAUGE metric descriptor, we know that startTime
> needs to equal endTime. However if we want to enforce this client-side, we
> have to pull down the metric descriptor first, introducing an extra API
> call. So it's probably best to just leave most of the validation server
> side.
>
> Do we need an actual Label class or are dicts good enough? I think dicts
> are good enough.
>

Short answer: Yes https://pypi.python.org/pypi/google-cloud is compatible with https://pypi.python.org/pypi/protobuf

----

We've seen a decent number of issues like this. It's unfortunate, but there isn't much we can do. It's either due to

- `pip` / `setuptools` mishandling an upgrade of a namespace package
- an older version of https://pypi.python.org/pypi/protobuf installed (older versions don't use)
- you have a different instal (e.g. TensorFlow) which is not properly a namespace package

Note, we [depend on][1] `protobuf` in our `google-cloud-core` package, which **all** the services depend on.

----

I recommend starting with a fresh environment and installing our package from PyPI, not from source:

```
(venv) $ pip install --upgrade google-cloud
```

----

To figure out what may be going wrong with the `google` namespace or the `google.cloud` namespace package, you'll need to import those objects and inspect the `__file__` and/or `__path__` attributes.

----

The namespace collision referenced is from 2014 and has been resolved for more than a year.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/3d28a5ceb1cba844e11710c98eac7933fbb4e977/core/setup.py#L56

@tboquet: I'm not sure I understand you, but if you don't want the dependencies serialized, then you don't want `recurse=True` -- the whole point there is to serialize the dependencies. If you have the dependencies in another file versus the same file, they also will serialize differently.  It's actually the easiest if the dependencies are in another installed module -- if that's the case, they will be pickled and unpickled by reference.  If the dependency modules are not installed (i.e. just other scripts in the same directory), then that doesn't work well (see issue #176, #123, etc)… and it's better to put the dependencies in the same file.

We can query studio for all public channels via Kolibri Studio's `api/public/channels`

To determine whether a given channel is unlisted, we would need to make one request to `api/public/channels/lookup` to verify that it is an unlisted channel. So, to implement this, we would go through the difference of Installed Channels and Public Channels and do this query for each one.

I've asked some people if they could simply annotate the Channel, post-installation as a non-public channel, so it is available immediately and we don't need to query Studio.

@indirectlylit I think one use case is to get the root id of the channel after downloading the database of the channel from studio. For example, when we do a network import, we call `/api/remotechannel/` to get the list of public channels. After that, if we select one channel, then the database of the channel will be downloaded. Later we want to show the contentnodes under the channel, so we need to call `/api/contentnode_granular/<root_node_id>` to get the top level contentnodes. However, the studio api `/api/remotechannel` doesn't come with the root id, so we need to get it from `/api/channel/<channel_id>` where this channel doesn't have content available. If we only show available channels for `/api/channel` then I guess we are not able to get the root id there in this case.

One way of implementing collection building in the IPFS could be as follows:

* start with the well known starting point `/about` (which would be under the namespace of individuals/organizations for the authorization purposes) and use a URN to identify the collection like `/about/urn:archive-collections:my%20collection`
* this collection node would keep a list of reference to about URI-R (illustrated as `/about/{URI}` in earlier diagrams) nodes like `["/about/example.com", "/about/cnn.com", "..."]`
* keeping the list inside the collection node itself will make sure that the history of the evolving collection seeds is also being archived and old versions of the collection manifest are also accessible via IPNS
* note that we did not use plain list of URI-Rs to define the collection like `["example.com", "cnn.com", "..."]` because we want collections to be scoped and not behave like any memento of a given URI-R is part of the collection no matter who archived it; at the same time this style would allow collection builders to pick and choose which namespaces (one or more) should be included in the collection

Let me describe a bit more explanation about the well know entry point `/about`. It will not be something available in the global namespace where anyone can write, instead, every participant in the IPFS will have an identity (which could be a social network handle, an IPFS block hash, a blockchain address such as namecoin or whatnot) and everyone's `/about` entry point will be under their respective namespace. This will make sure that people/organizations can participate in web archiving and collection building while utilizing shared deduplication and at the same time the job of web archive aggregators will be safe.

Live site is what it is in this very moment. You have a past capture of the page. You will know how it looked when it was captured after you will replay it.

What is the status here? I agree with @yashu-seth that this `get_parameters` should be fixed. The second bug isn't hard to fix, it just requires a decision. 

I'll try to summarize the issues again. I think @yashu-seth pointed to two independent bugs with `get_parameters()`:
- Some exception that wasn't easy to reproduce. I think this is the windows issue #602, and it is addressed in #604, so it might be solved already (can't check, no windows). Will definitely be solved in case pandas is not used (#594), and this is being worked on. (there is PR #596 which proposes to reimplement pandas DataFrame.)
- A bug about the cardinality of the CPDs. This is the very same issue as #352 and #573. This is the issue I'd like to see fixed. I'll describe it below:

**Problem:**
An exception occurs in `get_parameters()` if the data set does not include at least one sample for *every* configuration that the parents of any variable may have.
**Minimal example:**
```python
>>> from pandas import DataFrame
>>> from pgmpy.models import BayesianModel
>>> from pgmpy.estimators import MaximumLikelihoodEstimator
>>> m = BayesianModel([('A','C'), ('B','C')])
>>> d = DataFrame(data={'A':[0,0,1], 'B':[0,1,0], 'C':[1,1,0]})
>>> d
   A  B  C
0  0  0  1
1  0  1  1
2  1  0  0
>>> cpds = MaximumLikelihoodEstimator(m,d).get_parameters()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/lodrik/tmppgmpy/pgmpy/pgmpy/estimators/MLE.py", line 75, in get_parameters
    evidence_card=parent_card.astype('int'))
  File "/home/lodrik/tmppgmpy/pgmpy/pgmpy/factors/CPD.py", line 141, in __init__
    super(TabularCPD, self).__init__(variables, cardinality, values.flatten('C'))
  File "/home/lodrik/tmppgmpy/pgmpy/pgmpy/factors/Factor.py", line 106, in __init__
    raise ValueError("Values array must be of size: {size}".format(size=np.product(cardinality)))
ValueError: Values array must be of size: 8
```
 
The error goes away if we add an additional data sample where `A` and `B` are both `1`:
```python
>>> from pandas import DataFrame
>>> from pgmpy.models import BayesianModel
>>> from pgmpy.estimators import MaximumLikelihoodEstimator
>>> m = BayesianModel([('A','C'), ('B','C')])
>>> d = DataFrame(data={'A':[0,0,1,1], 'B':[0,1,0,1], 'C':[1,1,0,0]})
>>> d
   A  B  C
0  0  0  1
1  0  1  1
2  1  0  0
3  1  1  0
>>> cpds = MaximumLikelihoodEstimator(m,d).get_parameters()
>>> print(cpds[0])
╒═════╤═════╕
│ A_0 │ 0.5 │
├─────┼─────┤
│ A_1 │ 0.5 │
╘═════╧═════╛
>>> print(cpds[2])
╒═════╤═════╕
│ B_0 │ 0.5 │
├─────┼─────┤
│ B_1 │ 0.5 │
╘═════╧═════╛
>>> print(cpds[1])
╒═════╤═════╤═════╤═════╤═════╕
│ A   │ A_0 │ A_0 │ A_1 │ A_1 │
├─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┤
│ B   │ B_0 │ B_1 │ B_0 │ B_1 │
├─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┤
│ C_0 │ 1.0 │ 1.0 │ 0.0 │ 0.0 │
├─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┤
│ C_1 │ 0.0 │ 0.0 │ 1.0 │ 1.0 │
╘═════╧═════╧═════╧═════╧═════╛
```

**Solution:**
@yashu-seth spotted the issue in [line 72](https://github.com/pgmpy/pgmpy/blob/dev/pgmpy/estimators/MLE.py#L72), where the resulting value table does not contain a column for every *possible* configuration of the parents, but only for those which are *observed* in the sample.

To fix this we just have to extend the value table to include conditional probabilities for all states the parents variables may have (# of columns should the the product of the cardinalities of the parents).

The only question is: **What conditional probabilities should a Maximum Likelihood estimator estimate if the sample size (for a particular configuration of parents) is zero?** :D  It is a bit arbitrary, but I would suggest uniform probabilities. So in the example of a binary variable `C` above, if no data was observed for the case `A=1`, `B=1`, then `P(C=0|A=1,B=1)=P(C=1|A=1,B=1)=1/2`.

So we just have to complete the value table from [line 72](https://github.com/pgmpy/pgmpy/blob/dev/pgmpy/estimators/MLE.py#L72) by adding a column for each missing parent configuration with uniform values `1/card` (where `card` is the number of states the current variable can take).

Hi @achourasia,

I'm assuming you're talking about a URL that points into the Transfer page of the Globus web app?

The CLI doesn't provide a way to generate that URL but you could do it yourself fairly easily:

The base URL should be: `https://www.globus.org/app/transfer`.
And the query string parameters are as follows:

* `origin_id` (left file browser), `destination_id` (right file browser) is contains the UUID id of the endpoint.
* `origin_path`, `destination_path` are the URL-encoded paths that you want to browse to initially.

Hope that helps.

For anything north of 20 s or so (and excluding the time during download and extraction of packages), I agree it's likely time spent by the solver.

> I'd be intrigued to know why what takes you 5s takes me 11s. 

That's likely because I didn't include `conda-forge` in the channel list. `conda-forge` is a *huge* channel which gets updated very frequently. Hence, usually `conda` will need to download and parse the channel's repository index. The is some room for performance improvements regarding the parsing, but that might mean working against the generic and tidy concept that code uses, which wouldn't be very nice. For overall performance improvements, there are other parts (mainly solver) that provide more potential for gains :wink:.

See the following output for execution times that nearly solely consist of index updates -- without and with `conda-forge` added.
(Those `CONDA_CHANNELS=` lines are to preload the less often updated parts of the `defaults` channel.)
The gist is that adding `conda-forge` adds approx. 6 seconds to the index update phase -- which is exactly the 5 to 11 seconds delta. Looks like our setups perform surprisingly similarly :smile:

Taking the VM creation example, since that's probably a pretty well used command, there are a number of other resources it's validating or creating. So it would nice to at least see, "yep, storage found", "yep, vnet and nic found", "now creating VM", etc. Especially since you're waiting on response from the underlying APIs each time. Maybe this goes under a more readable output from `--verbose`.

If I'm just creating a new VNet, there's probably not a lot I'm waiting on, and it should be a pretty quick operation. I don't think anything needs to be displayed until the operation has completed. Same with creating a new storage account, or a new public IP address, etc. 

But on those longer-running operations, like creating a VM or deploying from a template, it would be nice to get an indication rather than nothing really being displayed for minutes until the whole operation has completed. Then you can at least see, "OK, all the supporting resources are in place, now it's actually churning on spinning up the new VM"


Actually, on the template deployment, bonus points if that then notes the deployment has been handed off, and is in progress - the existing node CLI returns you to the prompt within a few seconds of starting a new deployment (`azure group create --template-uri azuredeploy.json`) and says `Provisioning State: Succeeded` when really that's acknowledging the platform accepted the deployment task, not having finished creating all the actual resources within the template :) 

Double bonus points for being able to then track that deployment progress. But we're getting outside the scope of this issue and is handled elsewhere, really!

@judy , sorry for the inconvenience. The fundamental issue with this is like I said - a specific version of sfctl is supposed to work on a corresponding version of cluster or above nowadays which is our "backward compatibility" guarantee. What sfctl is doing is calling cluster's REST API with a specific "api-version" in the body. That's why your sfctl can not help you upgrading v5.7 cluster.

By "trying to get the cmdlets working" you mentioned, did you mean you tried PowerShell? Is your cluster a secured cluster or no?

We will improve the following aspects:
1. Add a switch in sfctl to allow overriding "api-version" which allows talking to older version of cluster.
2. The cluster should return a verbose error message to tell the request is rejected because of version not supported and which version is the latest it can support.
Also a parameter named `--app-id` is not in line with the convention in other commands as they use `application-id` parameter name.

As another aside, suppose you have a long-running process that is updating many documents and such, and the client is awaiting a response.  If the client unexpectedly disconnects in the middle, the recovery cases are insane since it could stop your job at literally any line in the code -- you have to guard that literally every single line could be a failure point. > @bennuttall is there a way the piwheels infra can always rebuild arm6 wheels

There's no need to rebuild all wheels, because "armv6" and "armv7" wheels are identical when built on a Pi. It's only when there's an armv7 wheel on PyPI (this is the first one I've seen, as it's a [relatively new addition](https://github.com/pypa/warehouse/issues/2003) that causes an issue).

It may be that we can check the wheel downloaded and force a rebuild when we see it's armv7. See https://github.com/bennuttall/piwheels/issues/66

Just providing some background on what this will entail:

# Background

The consensus hash is a mechanism that partitions set of (non-enumerable) Blockstack nodes into fork-sets.  Each Blockstack node assigns each block a consensus hash that is cryptographically derived from the sequence of name operations it has processed.  If two Blockstack nodes have the same view of the blockchain, and follow the same rules, they will each derive the same sequence of consensus hashes (w.h.p).  Conversely, if two Blockstack nodes do NOT have the same view of the blockchain, or do NOT follow the same rules, they will derive a DIFFERENT sequence of consensus hashes (w.h.p) from the block at which they disagree, and forever after (w.h.p).  This is what makes Blockstack nodes fork*-consistent.

When a Blockstack client announces a name operation, it selects which fork-set the name operation will be destined for by including a *recent* consensus hash in the name operation.  This way, only nodes in the same fork-set as the client will consume the name operation.  Nodes in other fork-sets will ignore it.

# Problem Statement

Names can be owned by multi-sig addresses, and the keys represented by those addresses can be held by different parties.  In order to send a name operation, the parties must each sign a name operation derived from a *recent* consensus hash.  This significantly limits the amount of time the parties have in order to generate the name operation.  The current implementation mandates that consensus hashes are valid for 24 blocks, or about 4 hours.  For systems like Misthos, this means that owners of a named venture would need to be online within the same 4 hour window in order to issue a name operation.  This leads to a bad user experience.

# Proposed Solution

Instead of mandating that a consensus hash is valid for 24 blocks globally, we make the number of blocks configurable on a per-namespace basis.  A namespace could allow a name operation to use a consensus hash older (or younger) than 24 blocks and still be valid.

The biggest consequence of this solution is that in the event of there being multiple emerging fork-sets, a name operation could be valid in all of them for a much longer period of time than 24 blocks.  In other words, operations in one fork-set could be replayed in other fork-sets.  This applies to operations generated *before* the conflicting fork-sets emerged, but got delayed in the mempool so that they are processed in both fork-sets after the fork occurs.  Clients that are aware of the emerging fork-sets would avoid this by selecting a newer consensus hash that occurs *later* in the desired fork-set's history than the fork itself, so other fork-sets will (rightfully) ignore the name operation.

The consensus hash validity window would only affect name operations and (should it be implemented) namespace-specific tokens. It would *not* affect the consensus hash check for the Stacks token.

# Alternatives

## Subdomains

The motivation for this solution is that names can be owned by multiple people who need to coordinate updates in a timely fashion.  This could also be addressed by using a subdomain name instead of an on-chain name.

Unlike on-chain names, subdomains do not require consensus hashes to update.  An update can be signed off on by each key holder over an arbitrarily long timescale.  Moreover, updating and transferring subdomains is considerably cheaper if done in batches with other subdomains.

If a trusted subdomain registrar is available for broadcasting the signed updates and transfers, then this problem can be addressed today.

## Shared Records in Gaia

In the context of application-specific assets owned by multiple parties, an alternative to using the on-chain naming system for binding assets to users is to implement named shared records at the application level.  A shared record would only be considered valid by the application if it was signed by a requisite set of keys (also determined by the application).

For example, `alice.id` could create a shared venture file in her Gaia bucket for Misthos that `bob.id` and `carol.id` can read, and must sign in order for the Misthos client to consider valid.  To do this, the Misthos client would allow both `bob.id` and `carol.id` to read the venture, and sign and store their respective signatures over the shared venture in their respective Gaia buckets.  Later, `alice.id` would copy and retain a record of their signatures.  If desired, any of the participants can notarize the signatures via a notary.

1. I added basic synchronization to the client using a simple decorator (`def _synchronize`). However, I don't have a good way to test client sychronization at the moment (it turns out this is kinda hard to do in Python).
2. In light of @joel-coffman's suggestions about the interface, I've gone back to the original client operation method names (e.g., `create` instead of `create_symmetric_key`); I like these names more for a KMIP interface than the new ones I was using. As for custom attributes, I'm reluctant to add them in to PyKMIP core. By definition a custom attribute needs its own tag(s) and primitive object definitions and it won't be recognized as a standard data type by a vanilla KMIP appliance. > The value of fold will also be ignored whenever a timedelta is added to or subtracted from a datetime instance which may be either aware or naive. I suppose I was worried about counter-intuitive behavior with your implementation, but I guess all that counter-intuitive behavior is *part of the spec*. I believe that this is not a problem. The first rule requests the set of all Sundays in a given year (there should be 52 or 53 of these), then selects the one 13th from the end.

The second rule requests the set of all days which are exactly 182 days after Easter (which happens to be the 13th Sunday from the end, in 2015).

The third rule requests the list of all Sundays which are exactly 182 days after Easter (there is exactly one of these), and chooses the 13th one from the end of that set - but the set is exactly 1 element long, so in any given year, there will never be a 13th-from-the-end Sunday-which-is-exactly-182-days-after-Easter. Thus, the rule is empty.

The problem is now that a folium.Map (the old, legacy object) is inheriting from folium.map.Map (the new object, the one we want to keep). Having different, similar objects under the same name causes confusion and can determine name clashes.

Two solutions I think of:
- We can rename folium.map.Map -> folium.map.LegacyMap and have folium.map.Map inherit from it. When we want to drop the deprecated methods we remove the inheritance. 
- We merge the objects (keeping deprecated methods together in the same section) and when we want to drop the deprecated methods we just delete their lines. :smile:

I tried the GRUDenseLayer approach for a project i'm working on. So far i think

* We can get rid of all the stacking of params code 
* Remove all code related to add gate params
* Remove all the ugly non_seqs code and just replace it with *args


```Python
        def step(input_n, hid_previous, *args):
            hid_input = get_output(self.hid_to_rnn, hid_previous)
            if not self.precompute_input:
                input_n = get_output(self.input_to_rnn, input_n)

            # Reset and update gates
            resetgate = slice_w(hid_input, 0) + slice_w(input_n, 0)
            updategate = slice_w(hid_input, 1) + slice_w(input_n, 1)
            resetgate = self.nonlinearity_resetgate(resetgate)
            updategate = self.nonlinearity_updategate(updategate)

            # Compute W_{xc}x_t + r_t \odot (W_{hc} h_{t - 1})
            hidden_update_in = slice_w(input_n, 2)
            hidden_update_hid = slice_w(hid_input, 2)
            hidden_update = hidden_update_in + resetgate*hidden_update_hid
            if self.grad_clipping is not False:
                hidden_update = theano.gradient.grad_clip(
                    hidden_update, -self.grad_clipping, self.grad_clipping)
            hidden_update = self.nonlinearity_hid(hidden_update)

            # Compute (1 - u_t)h_{t - 1} + u_t c_t
            hid = (1 - updategate)*hid_previous + updategate*hidden_update
            return hid
```

I managed to get that done for the AHI HSD reader. Replacing the ``open()`` calls was straightforward, but it turned out that ``numpy.fromfile`` and ``numpy.memmap`` do not support ``io.BytesIO`` buffers. Fortunately somebody found a workaround (https://github.com/adamreeve/npTDMS/pull/48). The memory map is essentially replaced by a simple array, but that doesn't matter because the whole file is already in memory anyway.

I tried to refactor the file handler class so that it is easier to subclass: https://github.com/pytroll/satpy/compare/master...sfinkens:ahi_hsd_bz2

Based on that class you can define your own customization:

    from satpy.readers.ahi_hsd import AHIHSDBaseFileHandler
    import numpy as np
    import io
    import bz2file
    
    
    class bz2open(object):
        """Decompress multi-stream bz2 compressed files into memory"""
        def __init__(self, filename, size=-1):
            """
            :param filename: bz2 compressed file to be opened
            :param size: Number of bytes to be decompressed. If negative: Read the
            whole file.
            """
            self.filename = filename
            self.size = size
    
        def __enter__(self):
            """
            :return: File-like io.BytesIO buffer
            """
            self.decomp_file_handle = io.BytesIO(
                bz2file.BZ2File(self.filename).read(self.size))
            return self.decomp_file_handle
    
        def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
            self.decomp_file_handle.close()
    
    
    class AHIHSDBz2FileHandler(AHIHSDBaseFileHandler):
        def _open(self, filename, mode='r', size=-1):
            """Open a bz2 compressed file and decompress the given number of
            bytes into memory."""
            return bz2open(filename, size=size)
    
        def _read_header(self, file_handle, dtype, count):
            """Wrapper around numpy.fromfile to support BytesIO buffers.
    
            Copied from https://github.com/adamreeve/npTDMS/pull/48
            """
            return np.fromstring(file_handle.read(count * dtype.itemsize),
                                 dtype=dtype, count=count)
    
        def _read_data(self, filename, file_handle, dtype, shape):
            """numpy.memmap does not support BytesIO buffers.
            Workaround: Create a simple numpy array from the buffer."""
            num_elem = np.array(shape, dtype='i8').prod()
            return self._read_header(file_handle, dtype=dtype, count=num_elem).reshape(shape)

- `_swt.py` : `swt`
- `_multidim.py` : `swt2`, `swtn`

You also can’t subclass `Union`, because it’s metaclass determines what options are necessary (or superfluous), and so you have to subclass `UnionMeta`. Then, however, the connection field is unhappy because your custom type isn’t a subclass of Union type (i.e. an exception is raised).

I have since looked into this, and I have decided to not do this. In short: you were right, the XML-based format is not suitable for a straightforward implementation of synchronisation. More specifically, because the XML-based format does not contain the last modification timestamp for each item, it is not possible to do two-way synchronisation in a sensible way. Of course, it is possible to modify the format that tudu uses, but if we're going to modify tudu, we might as well modify it to use the native VTODO-format.

I think you only get guaranteed order of acquisitions if you turn off removal and block on the lock. We never block on the lock due to our use of `fcntl.LOCK_NB` ([source1](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/4449/files#diff-7b5b97de8a6b56007042c2315622399aR77), [source2](https://docs.python.org/2/library/fcntl.html#fcntl.lockf)). If you don't block, you're executing non-atomic Python code before you attempt to acquire the lock again so I don't see how you get a guaranteed order here.

Eduard Braun:
> This is a welcome change but I think this needs some discussion:
> 

Ok :)

> #### Why is it slow in the first place?
> Have you determined what causes the overhead when calling Scour for each file individually?  

I have not.  However, my concrete usage for scour was to process the
auto-generated SVG graphs from gnuplot on a static generated website.
That site contains about ~1000 SVG files, even "small" things add up here.



> * Is there anything slow in Scour or is it simply calling Python?

It would seem that python is a significant part of it (although not the
only "slowness").  On my machine, an empty program (via -c '') costs
about 0.04s / 40ms.  For 1000 SVG files that becomes 40 seconds

"""
$ time python3 -c ''

real    0m0.046s
user    0m0.036s
sys     0m0.011s
"""

On the performance case I mentioned in my commit, python3's start up
time would account for roughly 4s out of 13s (~1/3) of the overhead.

> * Are the 40% overhead you mentioned for real-world data or for "empty" SVG files? (i.e. does the overhead matter when operating on typical files?
> 

Before I started submitting patches, the SVG files would take about
300ms a piece according to scour's own timing (i.e. excluding start up
time).  With the patches, I believe I got them down to about ~200ms a
piece in average.

What is a bit tricky is the timing in my patch is based on images that
were already scour'ed once (while the 300ms just above is not).

> If something is slow in Scour I'd prefer to optimize that first
> 

That is a good idea too.  However, for my use case, a bulk-processing
interface is going to be a significant improvement.

> #### How to specify output file names
> I'm not sure requiring a single output directory is the best way to approach this as it causes an asymmetry between processing single files and processing multiple files and would make it impossible to give precise file names to the output files.

I am happy to change the interface as long as it is compatible with
xargs.  For that, I basically need a static option in the beginning
(e.g. -o <dir>) and then be able to append "infinitely many input files
after that".

> In your particular implementation it's also unclear what input files are and what the output file is (e.g. what would happen if the user types `scour in1.svg in2.svg in3.svg`? Or for that matter only `scour in1.svg in2.svg`?)
> 

With the interface proposed then:

 * If you use -o, then that is always the output as always.
 * If there are multiple input files, then the output must be a
   directory.  There should be several checks to ensure this.

I would be perfectly fine with requiring -o for the output with multiple
input files if that would solve this concern for you.


> Some possible implementations I can think of right now:
> * Always expect groups of input/output files, i.e.  
>   `scour -i in1.svg -o out1.svg -i in2.svg -o out2.svg`  
>   which has the downside of requiring a lot of typing but would keep behavior consistent otherwise.

It would also make bulk processing via "xargs" or "find -exec ... +"
more difficult.  Those are the particular tools I am targeting.

> * Add separate options (e.g. `--outdir` and/or `--suffix`) that switch Scour into "batch processing mode" and deactivate the need for an output file name treating all unnamed parameters as input file names.  
>   This could also work well alongside an `--inplace` option that would then overwrite input files (#129).
> 
Indeed, this would work for me.  Personally I would prefer having an
output directory or --inplace as it makes easier for me (in my use case
I throw away the original files once  they have been scour'ed).

A third alternative is to have scour handle glob'ing itself.  That is,
you pass a literal *.svg to scour (e.g. \*.svg) and have scour expand it
internally.  Similar to many of git's commands.  That said, I fear
people will get this wrong more often than letting the shell expand the
glob.

> I'm also thinking about what should happen if a user calls scour like `scour *.svg`.
> 

If there is exactly two files, the user will trash one of the files
(with or without my proposed change).  As mentioned, I believe my
proposal handles that particular case (as the "last file" is unlikely to
be a directory).
  However, regardless of what route we choose, I think we should
definitely protect the user from accidentally trashing half of their svg
files if there are more than two.

IOW, if the user typed that (and there were more than two SVG files),
then scour ought to say "Did you want to use --in-place?" or "Missing
<option> to do bulk processing".

(The reason why I do not cover two files is that it is impossible for
scour to tell "scour in.svg out.svg" from "scour *.svg" apart if "*.svg"
expands to "in.svg out.svg").

@zakk0610 
In your example, that model does not meet the spec.
`Y = BN(X, scale, bias)    with shape_scale=(1, C, 1, 1), shape_bias=(1, C, 1, 1)`
scale and bias MUST have shape `(C,)`, otherwise, the model is wrong.

So, with correct model,
```
X = Conv ...              with shape_X = (N, C, H, W)
Y = BN(X, scale, bias)    with shape_scale=(C,), shape_bias=(C,)
Z = Mul(Y, A)             with shape_A=(1, C, 1, 1)  ; A is Tensor (weight) 
W = Add(Z, B)             with shape_B=(1, C, 1, 1)  ; B is Tensor (weight) 
```
We can't just update scale and bias in-place by using A and B now.
So I add squeeze and arithmetic node before BN.
```
X = Conv ...              with shape_X = (N, C, H, W)
NEW_A = Squeeze(A)    with shape_A=(1, C, 1, 1)  ; A is Tensor (weight), and shape_NEW_A=(C,)
NEW_B = Squeeze(B)    with shape_B=(1, C, 1, 1)  ; B is Tensor (weight), and shape_NEW_B=(C,)
NEW_SCALE = Mul(scale, NEW_A)      scale is original scale, shape_scale=(C,), shape_NEW_SCALE=(C,)
NEW_BIAS = Mul(bias, NEW_A)
NEW_BIAS = Add(B, NEW_BIAS)    bias is original bias, shape_bias=(C,), shape_NEW_BIAS=(C,)
Y = BN(X, NEW_SCALE, NEW_BIAS)
```
